speaker: interviewer
text: The survivor is George Gottlieb. It is September 24, 1996. Tape six. Who do we have in this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's my maternal grandparents, Miksa Kohn and his wife Antonia, my grandmother, whom I never had a chance to meet them. This picture was taken in 1910. I was born in '29. And they passed away in 1925 and '26 respectively.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was--

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my-- my mom's parents. And as I haven't got a picture of my mother, they represent how she stays forever with us, with me.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was taken in Hungary, in Acs, A-C-S, where my mother was born. That's the place where they lived.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is in this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my grandfather, Lipot Gottlieb, my father's father, who lived with us. And this picture was taken approximately on his 70th birthday in Budapest.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is in this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my father, Imre. That's the only picture what I have, what I could get from one of his buddies, Army buddies from the First World War, as an officer in the Hungarian Army. That's the only picture what I have of him.

speaker: interviewer
text: And it was taken in?

speaker: interviewee
text: Around 1918, '20.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Hungary.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who do we have in this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my brother, Louis. And this picture was taken sometimes in 1943, probably, because the age, approximately.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Budapest, in Hungary.

speaker: interviewer
text: And in this picture we have?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's me. Sometimes, has to be around the 1930s, by looking at the size what I am. Dressed up for Purim. And the picture was taken and I found this picture at one of my auntie's house. And surprisingly, nobody else in the-- from the family, not from my mom, dad, or-- that's a picture what she had.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Bicske, in Hungary. At our home.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is a picture of?

speaker: interviewee
text: My brother and I, sometimes 1942 or '43, winter, in Budapest, on the-- on the ski hill, going to try to ski.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where, Mr. Gottlieb, did you obtain most of these pre-war pictures?

speaker: interviewee
text: These pictures-- this particular picture, I got from a friend of ours who lived in Budapest, survived. He had the picture. I-- the other pictures, as I explained to you, very few of them what-- any other way what I have, than through family, or et cetera.

speaker: interviewer
text: And here, we have?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's an invitation for an unveiling of a memorial which was placed in the cemetery in Bicske, by the survivors, by us who survived, to memorialize those who perished in Auschwitz-Birkenau, from our town of Bicske, which is the memorial you're going to see next to it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Here we have?

speaker: interviewee
text: The memorial, which is erected in Bicske in 1947, September, to memorialize all those who perished in Auschwitz. And by the very few-- by the very few of us who remained. And if you can-- on the top of the list, you see there my-- my grandfather and our grandmother-- my grandfather and my father's name, who were respectively the president of the synagogue and president of the school board, and basically the elders of town with the rabbi on top of it-- Rabbi [? Frankel. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: We're looking, here, at?

speaker: interviewee
text: At the top of the memorial, where you can see my grandfather's, and the rabbi, and the other elder of the synagogue's name. And under it, all the rest of the families who perished.

speaker: interviewer
text: In this picture, we have?

speaker: interviewee
text: The four musketeers who took care of our children. With the glasses is Yitzak Weiss, then it's myself, and then it's Menachum Weiss, and Uri from Anatoli on the right-hand side. We were in charge of four kids. This picture was taken in 1940-- probably '46 or beginning of '47, in Bayerisch Gmain. Sometime, I shall put on food for them.

speaker: interviewer
text: [LAUGHS] In this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: These are my little kids. This also taken in Bayerisch Gmain. You, you can see me with my at that time, my hair cut, a little bit. Sitting in the middle. These are the kids who came, basically, all from behind the Iron Curtain and eventually wound up in Palestine and Israel [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: And here we have?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my identification card from Ort what shows that I'm a teacher for Ort, although my being the only job what I had is to take care of the children. And this basically let us be legitimized a little bit, our existence. This was a wonderful opportunity to-- to have.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what do we have here?

speaker: interviewee
text: These are emblems what we [? sew-- ?] sew on our uniforms, which were basically American uniform. And naturally, they are all false. And they more or less helped us to Americanize ourself and cross the borders and-- and-- and-- from Germany into Czechoslovakia into Hungary. And that's what helped us to-- to exist and not to be arrested.

speaker: interviewee
text: And here, we have? The pride and joy of mine, the three kids. From left to right, on your left with the blue shirt is Evan. Evan, he's the older, and the big guy. And the-- absolutely super, super proud of all three of them. And I think so the biggest-- biggest achievement of my lifetime was that I was able to achieve what my parents couldn't see, all three of them graduating from college. And in the right is Lawrence. Lawrence graduated from law school. And in the middle is the-- the lady of the house, Randi, who graduated from Syracuse. And all three of them are the most loving, wonderful, wonderful children that the parents can just dream of. But I love them, sincerely.
